Description: Count to 100 by ones and tens. Count forward beginning from a given number within the known sequence.
Write numbers from 0 to 20. Represent a number of objects with a written numeral 0-20.
Use place value understanding to compose and decompose numbers from 11-19.
Identify, write, represent, and compare numbers up to 20.
Model and solve addition and subtraction situations with totals within 10 using objects, drawings, or equations.
Explain, extend, and create repeating patterns with a repetition, not exceeding 4 and describe patterns involving time.
Observe, describe, and compare the physical and measurable attributes of objects and analyze graphical displays of data.
Identify, describe, and compare basic shapes encountered in the environment, and form 2D and 3D figures.
Count, write, and represent numbers up to 120; understand place value of tens and ones.
Use part-whole strategies, properties of operations, and a number line to add and subtract within 20.
Apply properties of operations and part-whole strategies to add and subtract within 100.
Identify, describe, extend, and create growing and shrinking patterns.
Measure lengths indirectly and by iterating length units; represent and interpret data.
Tell and write time in hours and half-hours using analog and digital clocks.
Compose and decompose 2D and 3D shapes; partition circles and rectangles into halves and quarters.
Explain the value of a three-digit number using hundreds, tens, and ones in a variety of ways.
Count forward and backward by ones from any number within 1000. Count by fives, tens, and hundreds.
Represent, compare, and order whole numbers to 1000 with an emphasis on place value and equality.
Fluently add and subtract within 20 using a variety of mental, part-whole strategies.
Find 10 more or 10 less than a given three-digit number and find 100 more or 100 less.
Solve problems involving the addition and subtraction of two-digit numbers using part-whole strategies.
Fluently add and subtract within 100 using strategies based on place value and properties of operations.
Work with equal groups to gain foundations for multiplication through real-life, mathematical problems.
Identify, describe, extend, and create repeating patterns, growing patterns, and shrinking patterns.
Estimate and measure the lengths of objects to solve problems found in real-life using standard units.
Analyze graphical displays of data (bar graphs, pictographs) to answer relevant questions.
Solve real-life problems involving time to the nearest five minutes.
Solve real-life problems involving money, using dollar bills, quarters, dimes, nickels, and pennies.
Describe, compare and sort 2-D and 3-D shapes given a set of attributes.
Identify at least one line of symmetry in everyday objects to describe each object as a whole.
Partition circles and rectangles into two, three, or four equal shares (halves, thirds, fourths).
Recognize that equal shares of identical wholes may be different shapes within the same whole.
Use place value understanding to round whole numbers to the nearest 10 or 100.
Interpret products and quotients of whole numbers; solve multiplication and division word problems within 100.
Understand fractions as numbers; represent fractions on a number line.
Explain equivalence of fractions and compare fractions by reasoning about their size.
Identify arithmetic patterns (including patterns in the addition table or multiplication table) and explain them using properties of operations.
Solve problems involving measurement and estimation of intervals of time, liquid volumes, and masses of objects.
Understand area as an attribute of plane figures and measure area by counting unit squares or multiplying side lengths.
Recognize that in a multi-digit whole number, a digit in one place represents ten times what it represents in the place to its right.
Fluently add and subtract multi-digit whole numbers using the standard algorithm.
Explain why a fraction a/b is equivalent to a fraction (n x a)/(n x b) by using visual fraction models.
Find all factor pairs for a whole number in the range 1-100; recognize prime and composite numbers.
Solve problems involving measurement and conversion of measurements from a larger unit to a smaller unit.
Recognize angles as geometric shapes that are formed wherever two rays share a common endpoint; measure angles in degrees using a protractor.
Recognize that in a multi-digit number, a digit in one place represents 10 times as much as it represents in the place to its right and 1/10 of what it represents in the place to its left.
Add and subtract fractions with unlike denominators, including mixed numbers, by replacing given fractions with equivalent fractions.
Write, evaluate, and interpret numerical expressions with grouping symbols (parentheses, brackets, or braces).
Convert among different-sized standard measurement units within a given measurement system.
Graph points in the first quadrant of the coordinate plane, and interpret coordinate values of points in the context of the situation.
Solve relevant, mathematical problems involving operations with whole numbers, fractions, and decimal numbers.
Apply operations with whole numbers, fractions and decimals within relevant applications.
Solve a variety of problems involving whole numbers and their opposites; model rational numbers on a number line.
Solve a variety of contextual problems involving ratios, unit rates, equivalent ratios, and percentages.
Identify, write, evaluate, and interpret numerical and algebraic expressions as mathematical models to explain relevant situations.
Write and solve one-step equations and inequalities as mathematical models to explain authentic, realistic situations.
Solve practical problems involving area of polygons and volume of right rectangular prisms.
Solve relevant, mathematical problems, including multi-step problems, involving the four operations with rational numbers.
Use properties of operations, generate equivalent expressions and interpret the expressions to explain relevant contextual situations.
Represent authentic situations using equations and inequalities with variables; solve symbolically using properties of equality.
Recognize proportional relationships in relevant, mathematical problems; represent, solve, and explain these relationships with tables, graphs, and equations.
Solve practical problems involving angle measurement, circles, area of circles, and surface area of prisms and cylinders.
Using mathematical reasoning, investigate chance processes and develop, evaluate, and use probability models to find probabilities of simple events.
Distinguish between rational and irrational numbers using decimal expansion. Convert a decimal expansion which repeats eventually into a rational number.
Approximate irrational numbers to compare the size of irrational numbers, locate them approximately on a number line, and estimate the value of expressions.
Apply the properties of integer exponents to generate equivalent numerical expressions.
Use square root and cube root symbols to represent solutions to equations. Evaluate square roots of perfect squares and cube roots of perfect cubes.
Interpret solutions to linear equations and inequalities in one variable.
Solve linear equations and inequalities in one variable with rational number coefficients.
Graph proportional relationships, interpreting the unit rate as the slope of the graph.
Understand that a function is a rule that assigns to each input exactly one output.
Construct and interpret scatter plots for bivariate measurement data to investigate patterns of association between two quantities.
Understand that solutions to a system of two linear equations in two variables correspond to points of intersection of their graphs.
Explain a proof of the Pythagorean Theorem and its converse.
Apply the Pythagorean Theorem to determine unknown side lengths in right triangles in real-world and mathematical problems in two and three dimensions.
Apply the formulas for the volume of cones, cylinders, and spheres and use them to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
